claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to know about your child. 

**What is your child's age?**

This is important because:
- Children **under 16** automatically qualify (with a few other conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** generally don't qualify

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Thanks for helping me with this. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old and lives at home with you.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), his eligibility depends on whether he's in approved education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

This would include:
- Full-time non-advanced education (such as A-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ Level 3 or below)
- Approved training programs
- Courses at school or college (not university degree courses)

Or, if he recently left education:
- Has he left education or training within the last 20 weeks?

Please let me know Alex's current situation regarding education or training.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex isn't in college or anything like that. He's actually started an apprenticeship - he's training to become an electrician.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great that Alex is doing an apprenticeship! Apprenticeships can qualify as approved training for Child Benefit purposes.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check a few details about Alex's apprenticeship to make sure it qualifies:

**Important questions about the apprenticeship:**

1. **Is the apprenticeship employer-funded?** (Does his employer pay for the training or has he agreed to a future job with them as part of the arrangement?)

2. **How many hours per week** does Alex spend in supervised study/training (not just working, but actual training time with instruction)?

This is important because:
- If the apprenticeship is **employer-funded** or he has an **agreed future job** with the employer, it unfortunately doesn't qualify for Child Benefit
- The training must involve at least **12 hours per week of supervised study**
- Advanced qualifications (Level 4 and above) don't qualify, but most apprenticeships at electrician level would be Level 2-3 which do qualify

Can you tell me about these aspects of Alex's apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, I didn't realize that mattered! Yes, it's through his employer - they're paying for his training and he's working for them as part of the apprenticeship program. He spends quite a bit of time at the training college each week, probably around 15 hours or so with his instructors, but the employer is definitely funding it all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. Unfortunately, this is important and does affect Alex's eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

## Why You're Not Eligible

Even though Alex is 17 years old (within the qualifying age range of 16-19) and is spending around 15 hours per week in supervised training, the fact that his apprenticeship is **employer-funded** means it doesn't count as approved education or training for Child Benefit purposes.

According to Child Benefit rules, training courses that are:
- Funded by an employer, OR
- Taken with an agreement for future employment with the employer

...do not qualify as approved education or training.

## What This Means

While Alex's apprenticeship is excellent for his career development, it falls outside the scope of what qualifies for Child Benefit. This is a specific rule that applies to employer-funded training programs.

## Possible Future Eligibility

If Alex's circumstances change in the future (for example, if he were to enter non-advanced, full-time education such as A-levels or a Level 3 qualification not funded by an employer), you could potentially become eligible at that time.

I know this isn't the outcome you were hoping for, but I hope this explanation helps clarify the rules. If you have any questions or if Alex's circumstances change, please feel free to reach out again.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old (within the 16-19 age range). While he is in training as an apprentice electrician and spends approximately 15 hours per week in supervised study, the apprenticeship is employer-funded. According to Child Benefit rules, training courses funded by an employer or courses taken with an agreement for future employment with the employer do not count as approved education or training for Child Benefit purposes. Therefore, Alex does not meet the education/training requirement for 16-19 year olds.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who is in an employer-funded apprenticeship program. Despite meeting the age requirement and spending sufficient hours in supervised training, the employer-funded nature of the apprenticeship means it does not qualify as approved education or training under Child Benefit rules. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.reason: Child aged 16-19 is in employer-funded training or training with employer agreement for future job
response.guidance: Training courses funded by an employer or courses taken with an agreement for future employment with the employer do not count as approved education or training for Child Benefit purposes.
